news.title: Four Observations from the World Cup third place match
news.lede: The third place match of this World Cup was something else. Four goals from England in the first half made it seem that we were not going to see a close game, but France had something to say about it. We saw records matched and overcome, and two key goals in the Golden Boot race. This was the highest scoring match in this edition of the tournament — surpassing Germany’s 7-1 win over Curacao.

news.title: World Cup Watch Party: The Final!
news.lede: It all comes down to this. It’s a great day to be a Barça fan and a terrible day to be a Madridista. Out of 48 teams, only two remain: Argentina and Spain. Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal.

news.title: 📸 World Cup: how the final match ball changed, 1930 to 2026
news.lede: Nearly a century separates the brown ball used in the first World Cup final in 1930 from the one that will be used at the 2026 edition. From the first hand-stitched models to the sophisticated “Trionda Final,” a true concentration of technology designed down to the smallest detail, the paradigm shift is total. On the occasion of the blockbuster clash between Spain and Argentina, let’s go back in time to look at the evolution of this iconic object, from 1930 to today. Kick-off for the Spain–Argentina final is this Sunday at 9 p.m.

news.title: Football Daily | Give it up for Vozinha, Spence and the 2026 World Cup’s cult heroes
news.lede: Every World Cup produces cult heroes, icons who make it possible for us to sit back and start misty-eyed recollections by saying: “Do you remember Roger Milla/Saeed Al-Owairan/Keisuke Honda/Paul the Octopus?” Well, the Geopolitics World Cup has not let us down! Here are 10 cult heroes who’ve emerged from this tournament. Yaya Sithole: a catastrophic start as the South Africa midfielder’s error led to the first goal of the GWC against Mexico, compounded by a second-half red card.

news.title: Even Donald Trump Couldn’t Ruin the World Cup
news.lede: Donald Trump and his ally, FIFA president Gianni Infantino, have plumbed new depths with their political antics before and during the World Cup. But as David Goldblatt insists, they still couldn’t destroy the moments of collective joy it gives us. Football has the remarkable capacity to bring moments of resistance, of challenge, of joy, of beauty in an ugly world. (Julian Finney / FIFA via Getty Images).
